summary: Gruber follows up on his piece about 1Password's $620 million funding round, acknowledging the argument that built-in OS password management is pushing 1Password toward enterprise. He notes that longtime users are unhappy about the Electron-based Mac app, subscription-only pricing, and cloud-only vaults — all decisions that make sense for enterprise SaaS but alienate the original user base. He draws a parallel to Dropbox's transformation from a lightweight sync tool into bloated software. Despite his concerns, he expresses cautious hope that 1Password can navigate the enterprise transition without losing its Mac and iOS identity.
key_points: Built-in password management in OSes and browsers may be eroding 1Password's consumer market, making the enterprise pivot a business necessity
Longtime 1Password users are unhappy about Electron, subscription-only pricing, and cloud-only vaults — all enterprise-friendly decisions that hurt power users
1Password co-founder Karimov pushes back, noting founders retain majority ownership as a safeguard against pure revenue optimization
Gruber draws a cautionary parallel to Dropbox, which transformed from a beloved lightweight tool into bloated software after its own growth phase
Despite never using 1Password himself, Gruber roots for them while acknowledging the tension between VC-backed growth and product quality
money_quotes: Whether what 1Password is doing is smart business or not, there's no question that the longtime 1Password users I know personally are unhappy.

These are all decisions that make perfect sense for the enterprise SaaS world. It might not be feasible to move to the new model without spoiling what many 1Password users liked best about their old one.
This much investment and valuation is going to AWAKEN THE EVIL GOD OF ARPU and I fear 1Password will succumb to his demands.

Used to be just a folder that syncs, with lightweight Mac software that stayed out of the way and just did its job — keeping that one magic folder in sync. Now, well, it's neither lightweight nor stays out of the way.
If anyone can transition to a VC-backed enterprise model yet remain true to their Mac and iOS roots, maybe it's them.
key_insight: Enterprise pivots driven by VC funding create an inherent tension with the product qualities that made a company beloved by its original users, and the Dropbox precedent suggests the original users usually lose.
